name: Balkan Magic
description: Balkan Magic encompasses a diverse range of folk beliefs and practices rooted in the cultural traditions of the Balkan Peninsula. Its origins are deeply intertwined with pre-Christian Slavic paganism, ancient Roman influences, and Orthodox Christian traditions, resulting in a unique syncretic system. Practices range from divination and healing rituals to protective charms and curses, often involving the manipulation of natural elements, spirits (including the vila, rusalka, and vampir), and the power of words (incantations and spells). Cultural significance lies in its role in addressing everyday concerns such as health, love, prosperity, and protection from malevolent forces. Divination is crucial, used to predict the future and diagnose illnesses. Healing often involves herbal remedies and ritualistic cleansing. Belief in the evil eye ('urokliivo') is prevalent, necessitating protective amulets and rituals. The magic is often passed down through families, particularly women, and holds a significant place in rural communities, serving as a way to understand and interact with the unseen world. The blending of pagan and Christian elements creates a complex and fascinating tapestry of beliefs and practices, still observed in varying degrees across the region.
subtitle: Folk magic of the Balkans

keyMyths.title: The Vila
keyMyths.description: Vilas are female spirits inhabiting mountains and forests. They possess great power and can bring both good fortune and misfortune, often associated with healing and protection but also with revenge.

keyMyths.title: The Evil Eye (Urokljivo)
keyMyths.description: The evil eye is a curse cast by a malevolent glare, causing illness or misfortune. Protection against the evil eye is a common concern, leading to the use of amulets and rituals.

sources.author: Tatia Anašvili
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of magical practices in the Balkan region.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of magical practices in the Balkan region.

sources.author: Gail Kligman
sources.work: The Wedding of the Dead: Ritual, Poetics, and Popular Culture in Transylvania
sources.description: Examines funeral rituals and beliefs about the afterlife, relevant to Balkan magic.
sources.text: Examines funeral rituals and beliefs about the afterlife, relevant to Balkan magic.
